JavaScript Core (60) - ES6: let & const - Practical Tricks for let and const

Last chapter covered the basics of let and const. This time we get into some real-world stuff — the classic interview loop-with-setTimeout gotcha, why let fixes it, the closure hiding inside it, and what const actually locks down when it points at an object (plus how Object.freeze and 'use strict' fit in).
JavaScript Core Concepts (59) - ES6: let and const - The Basics

Time for a brand new chapter. From here on we start digging into ES6 syntax, beginning with let and const. We'll look at why var causes trouble with hoisting, redeclaration, and function scope, and how let and const fix all of that with block scope.
JavaScript Core Concepts (58): Where Property Descriptors Actually Show Up in Real Frameworks

We've spent a whole chapter on object property descriptors, so let's see where they actually matter in the real world. In this post I walk through Vue.js source code to show how Object.defineProperty powers two-way data binding — and why that makes property descriptors such an important concept to understand.
JavaScript Core (57): Getter and Setter — Assigning a Value Without Calling a Function

Next up is Getter and Setter. If you've already started learning Vue, this concept is going to be especially useful to you. We'll look at how to define getters and setters directly in an object literal, how to do the same thing with Object.defineProperty, and where this pattern actually shows up in the real world (hint: Vue's two-way binding).
JavaScript Core (56) - Property Enumeration and the Prototype: Why for...in Leaks Prototype Props

Time to circle back to prototypes and the enumeration stuff we've been talking about, because the two are tangled together. In this post I'll walk through how property enumeration and the prototype chain relate, why for...in surprisingly pulls in prototype properties, and how to stop it using Object.defineProperty or hasOwnProperty.
JavaScript Core (55) — Locking Objects Down: preventExtensions, seal, and freeze

Last time we looked at Object.defineProperty, which tweaks individual properties on an object. This time we go one level up and operate on the whole object — preventExtensions, seal, and freeze. I'll walk through what each one locks down, how they relate to each other, and why nested objects don't get frozen for free.
JavaScript Core (54) - Property Descriptors: The Hidden Settings on Every Object Property

Every property on an object carries a set of hidden settings called descriptors — whether it can be rewritten, deleted, or looped over. Once you get how these work, a lot of framework behavior suddenly makes a lot more sense. This post walks through configurable, enumerable, value, and writable, plus how to read and tweak them with Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptor and Object.defineProperty.
JavaScript Core (53) - Inheritance & the Prototype Chain - Chapter Exercise

This chapter is all about practice. When a concept hasn't quite clicked, drilling it with lots of hands-on practice, sharing, or even teaching it can really cement your understanding. Here I build out an inheritance and prototype-chain example using the old online game Ragnarok Online — base character, novice skills, and a job change into the WhiteSmith class.
JavaScript Core (52) — Inheritance & Prototype Chains: The Big-Picture View of the Prototype Chain and Constructors

This post zooms out and walks through the whole prototype chain as one picture. Think of it as a recap that ties together everything we've covered so far — how an instance points up to its constructor's prototype, how that prototype points up to the next one, and how every prototype has its own constructor until you finally hit null. We'll prove it all with real code.
JavaScript Core (51) - Inheritance & the Prototype Chain: Multi-Level Inheritance with Object.create

In this chapter we'll build our own prototype chain from scratch. We'll start with a simple constructor, see why it falls short, then use Object.create to set up multi-level inheritance — Dog inheriting from Animal. Along the way we'll cover why you still need call() to inherit the constructor, and why you have to put back Dog.prototype.constructor to keep the prototype chain intact.
